feedback.text: Hello! I'd like to mention the Stop Killing Games (SKG) initiative and its mission to address how producers/publishers of video games treat their products and consumers. The SKG initiative proposes that games at risk of being discontinued (e.g. taking down servers after ending support) should have a clear end-of-life plan to make it possible to retain access to them - which is arguably a basic consumer right. I believe inclusion of legislation towards that goal in the Digital Fairness Act is a natural step towards better consumer rights for digital ownership, both for EU citizens and worldwide. The SKG initiative names a few examples of video games being discontinued without regard for players who purchased them (notably the case of "The Crew"), but I wish to mention another example that is dear to me personally. A similar fate happened to the "Duelyst" game a few years ago, when its servers were taken down after the publisher deemed it no longer profitable to maintain. Only by cooperation of the fan community and the publisher, was the source code made public and recently adopted by fans to perpetuate the game with a fresh lease of life. I believe for the good of players, such practices should be widely encouraged, in part by at least basic legislation, which the Digital Fairness Act can provide. Disclaimer: there is a similar effort by EU citizens to introduce this via the Stop Destroying Videogames European Citizen Initiative.
